sonnet_review.biasedText: The year is 2025. You froze your eggs a decade ago, after your live-in boyfriend dumped you on your 30th birthday. You're about to start your fifteenth round of IVF, and if this one doesn't work, you have a color-coded list of surrogacy agencies to call.
sonnet_review.reason: This hypothetical opener appears in the article and uses exaggerated, emotionally charged setup to frame IVF/surrogacy negatively.
The author’s own phrase; “necromancy” is a highly loaded term that casts the tech in grotesque light.

sonnet_review.biasedText: The ad's suggestion that lost loved ones can be rendered obsolete in minutes by soulless video game characters is repulsive and deeply disrespectful to the memories of the dead.
sonnet_review.reason: Direct authorial judgment with strong pejoratives; clear subjective value-laden framing.

sonnet_review.biasedText: No one wants a creepy Minecraft Grandma.
sonnet_review.reason: Appears verbatim; presents a universal claim as fact without evidence.

sonnet_review.biasedText: We've been lusting after it since Eden, desiring to play at being God. It's the same insidious desire that drives many to assisted reproductive technologies.
sonnet_review.reason: Author imputes motives to broad groups using moral/religious framing; speculative about intent.

sonnet_review.biasedText: While IVF, gamete donation, and surrogacy are billed as compassionate treatments to help earnest couples struggling with fertility, they are often used to facilitate the lifestyles of women who could have had children naturally but chose to delay doing so by decades
sonnet_review.reason: This appears in the text and downplays medical uses while asserting a negative generalization about women’s motives.
Direct pejorative characterization of the AI avatar; subjective and dismissive.

sonnet_review.biasedText: The destructive methods of assisted reproductive technologies result in life being valued less, not more.
sonnet_review.reason: A broad causal/value claim presented declaratively without balancing context.

sonnet_review.biasedText: But they ultimately strip her of both body and soul, reducing her to a Tamagotchi pet.
sonnet_review.reason: Exists in the piece; metaphor and diction are strongly demeaning and subjective.

sonnet_review.biasedText: Twenty-first-century magicians promise to circumvent the physical design of the natural order, from childbearing to the sex binary. Like Worthy's app, their cheap attempts are abusing, not augmenting, the real thing.
sonnet_review.reason: Pejorative metaphors (“magicians,” “cheap attempts,” “abusing”) assert moral judgments as statements about reality.

gpt_review.biasedText: you can digitize your parents and create immortal AI clones of them that will haunt your children forever.
gpt_review.reason: Appears verbatim; “haunt your children forever” is fear-evoking hyperbole.

gpt_review.biasedText: This touching act of necromancy was possible
gpt_review.reason: “Necromancy” is an overtly loaded, moralizing term used by the author.

gpt_review.biasedText: The ad's suggestion that lost loved ones can be rendered obsolete in minutes by soulless video game characters is repulsive and deeply disrespectful to the memories of the dead.
gpt_review.reason: Direct authorial value judgment with strong pejoratives; stated as definitive.

gpt_review.biasedText: We've been lusting after it since Eden, desiring to play at being God.
gpt_review.reason: Religious/moral framing imputes motives broadly; interpretive and charged.

gpt_review.biasedText: they are often used to facilitate the lifestyles of women who could have had children naturally but chose to delay doing so by decades, thanks to the promised flexibility of reproductive tech.
gpt_review.reason: Generalizes motives and frequency without specific supporting evidence here.

gpt_review.biasedText: normalize a process that's anything but.
gpt_review.reason: Dismissive phrasing that asserts a judgment rather than neutrally describing surrogacy.

gpt_review.biasedText: Story after story of surrogacy gone wrong tells of vulnerabilities in the under-regulated industry that enable traffickers, surrogate abuse, and eugenic attitudes.
gpt_review.reason: Sweeping claim about prevalence/harm without citations in the passage; strong negative framing.

gpt_review.biasedText: he is still ripped away from his birth mother, with whom he's bonded for nine months.
gpt_review.reason: “Ripped away” is emotive, assuming harm and framing it as fact.

gpt_review.biasedText: It's not just grandma whose existence has been divorced from her physical body; with surrogacy and artificial wombs, mom's will be too.
gpt_review.reason: Dramatic metaphor (“divorced from her physical body”) conveys disapproval rather than neutral description.

gpt_review.biasedText: Just like the fad of mutilating your body to appear like the opposite sex, it's an attempt to circumvent the confines of our created bodies.
gpt_review.reason: Highly pejorative characterization (“fad,” “mutilating”) of gender-affirming care; moralized framing.

gpt_review.biasedText: the destructive methods of assisted reproductive technologies result in life being valued less, not more.
gpt_review.reason: Presents only harms and a definitive negative conclusion about ART’s value.

gpt_review.biasedText: reducing her to a Tamagotchi pet
gpt_review.reason: Demeaning analogy that trivializes the concept; clearly value-laden.

gpt_review.biasedText: Worthy's gimmick is easy to mock, but it's not the only counterfeit being marketed to us.
gpt_review.reason: “Gimmick” and “counterfeit” are explicitly dismissive value judgments.

gpt_review.biasedText: the hubris to think we can override it...cheap attempts are abusing, not augmenting, the real thing.
gpt_review.reason: Both phrases appear in close proximity; moralized language (“hubris,” “abusing”) frames tech negatively.

gpt_review.biasedText: Rebellion against the laws of the former is almost always a real rebellion against the latter.
gpt_review.reason: End-of-article religious framing casting technological choices as moral rebellion.
